title: Uttapam

ingredients.name: fermented idli-dosa batter
visual_checks: slightly over-fermented batter works best here
ingredients.notes: slightly over-fermented batter works best here

subs.name: Mix 1 cup rice flour, 0.25 cup urad-dal flour and 0.5 cup curd with enough water to make a thick batter; use immediately for an instant version.
subs.notes: tanginess comes from curd; no fermentation needed

subs.name: spring-onion
subs.notes: milder taste; add the green parts along with the white bulbs

subs.name: beetroot, finely grated
subs.notes: adds earthy sweetness and a vivid pink colour; squeeze out excess water before using

steps.text: Mix the chopped onion, tomato, green chilli, ginger and coriander leaves together in a small bowl. Season lightly with salt — this is your topping mix.
Stir salt into the batter. It should be thicker than dosa batter — if it is too runny, the uttapam will not hold its shape. Do not thin it.

Heat a tawa on medium flame until a droplet of water sizzles and evaporates. Pour a large ladle of batter onto the centre and let it spread on its own to about 15 cm — do not spread like a dosa. The thickness is part of the dish.
Immediately scatter a heaped spoonful of the topping mix over the wet surface and press gently with the back of the ladle to embed the vegetables. Drizzle 0.5 tsp oil around the edges.
Cover loosely with a lid or foil and cook on medium flame for 3–4 minutes until the base is golden and the surface is set and no longer shiny. The uttapam is ready to flip when the edges lift easily.

Flip and cook the topping side for 1 minute — the vegetables should soften slightly but not char. Serve warm with sambar and coconut chutney. Uttapam stays good for a few minutes, unlike dosa, making it ideal for feeding a family in batches.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: onion → spring-onion (milder taste; add the green parts along with the white bulbs); tomato → beetroot, finely grated (adds earthy sweetness and a vivid pink colour; squeeze out excess water before using); idli-dosa-batter → Mix 1 cup rice flour, 0.25 cup urad-dal flour and 0.5 cup curd with enough water to make a thick batter; use immediately for an instant version. (tanginess comes from curd; no fermentation needed)
